summary: Cantrill reflects on how Docker's explosive growth in 2014 validated Joyent's long-held belief in OS-based virtualization while presenting a challenge: Docker was Linux-only but Joyent ran SmartOS. The solution came through reviving Linux branded zones (enabling Linux binaries to run on illumos), building a Docker API endpoint for SmartDataCenter, and advancing network virtualization. These efforts culminated in open-sourcing SmartDataCenter and Manta, positioning Joyent to ride the Docker wave while maintaining their technical differentiation.
key_points: Docker's 2014 rise validated Joyent's core hypothesis that OS-based virtualization is the future of infrastructure
Reviving lx branded zones allowed Linux binaries to run on SmartOS, solving the Docker compatibility problem
Docker's remote API enabled Joyent to build an alternative implementation without breaking existing tooling
Open sourcing SmartDataCenter and Manta was a strategic response to Docker's rise and OpenStack's open source advantage
Network virtualization for containers emerged as a critical unsolved problem that Joyent aimed to address
money_quotes: The enthusiasm for Docker has been invigorating: it validates Joyent's core hypothesis that OS-based virtualization is the infrastructure substrate of the future.

This is yet another example of why we so fervently believe in open source: it allows for others to explore ideas that may seem too radical for commercial entities with mortgages to pay and mouths to feed.

Like much bringup work, the 64-bit work was excruciating because it was very hard to forecast: you can be one bug away from a functioning system or a hundred – and the only way to really know is to grind through them all.
With respect to containers, Joyent has been living in the future (which, in case it sounds awesome, is actually very difficult; being ahead of the vanguard is a decidedly mixed blessing).
While OpenStack has been accused of being 'a software particle-board designed by committee', SDC has been deliberately engineered based on our experience actually running a public cloud at scale.
key_insight: Being ahead of the technology curve is a mixed blessing - validation feels good, but the real work is building bridges between your vision and where the market actually is.
